summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on four years at Stripe, describing how his role evolved from hands-on Atlas work to cross-functional publishing and developer experience. He details the organizational challenges of hypergrowth, where the company grew from ~650 to ~3,000 employees while constantly reinventing its processes. He argues that Stripe's economic model is uniquely powerful because it compounds on the success of its customers, who themselves compound on their customers' success. The piece also candidly addresses his struggle with depression in 2020, the surprising value of corporate infrastructure he once dismissed, and how working at a prestigious company reshaped his network access in ways that feel meritocratically uncomfortable.
key_points: Hypergrowth means half your coworkers always have less than a year of experience, making organizational metawork a permanent tax on execution
Stripe's business model adds recursive loops on top of B2B SaaS: indexing on the count and success of customers who themselves index on their customers' success

Corporate functions like HR exist because scaling companies inevitably hit predictable challenges, and every startup that hires a VP of Sales is publishing an empirical result that the role is necessary
Operating cadence compounds continuously — consistently asking 'could we do that faster?' produces dramatic returns at surprisingly low cost
McKenzie's network access and professional credibility changed materially upon joining Stripe despite no corresponding change in skill, illustrating class dynamics within meritocracy
money_quotes: I pick a peer group wisely because you're giving them write access to both your conscious thoughts and your entire worldview.

There is a meritocracy in our class system and a class system in our meritocracy, and I feel that this is underexamined because the nuances discomfit both people who understand the nature of merit and those who understand the nature of class systems.

Every scaling startup is an experiment in empirical microeconomics research on 'What parts of the typical corporate form are necessary and which are pageantry which we only keep around due to anchoring, the sunk cost fallacy, and tradition?'
The returns to pushing your cadence to faster are everywhere and they compound continuously, for years.
A huge portion of the value creation of Silicon Valley is through directly intervening on the ambition of impressionable people.
Most organizations operate at nowhere near the frontier of their capabilities. That is a choice, and strikes me as a valid choice, but you can choose to move closer to the frontier, too.
I feel like I've done the best work of my career to date but that if it ever becomes the best work looking forward something went deeply off the rails.
The software economy is larger than you think, even after taking into account Patio11's Law.
key_insight: Stripe's economic model is recursively compounding — it indexes on the success of customers who themselves index on the success of their customers — and this structural advantage, combined with relentless operating cadence, explains why insiders consistently underpredict the company's trajectory.
